Can you share the benchmark numbers in Slack after this? I want to have them on record. Yeah, for sure, I'll post the before and after. Hey, sorry, can I jump in real quick? I keep getting these mobile bug reports and I'm wondering if the search change broke something. It shouldn't have. The API contract is exactly the same. What are you seeing? Yeah, we didn't change any response formats. Wait, how many reports are we talking? Like eight in the last two days. Enough to notice a pattern. Some users saying search results are empty sometimes, but it might be a totally different thing. Hmm, could be a timeout issue on the mobile client. Let me take a look. Could also be a connection pooling thing. Mobile clients drop connections more often. Good point, I'll check both. Let's figure that out quickly. We don't want mobile users having a bad experience. Yeah, let's talk about it after this. I'll send you the ticket numbers. Okay, Carol, you're up next? Oh, one more thing about the search launch—we should talk about how we tested it. What do you mean? So we did the shadow deployment, ran both old and new in parallel for two weeks, compared results, and it worked really well, like really well. Yeah, the shadow traffic approach is something we should use for all major backend changes. That's smart, like a dress rehearsal before opening night. How extra compute does that take? Not much, maybe a couple hundred bucks for two weeks. That's nothing compared to a production outage. Worth it. We caught three bugs during the shadow phase that would've been production incidents. One of them was a really nasty edge case with Unicode characters in search queries. Oh, that would've been bad. Three bugs that would've been incidents? Yeah, that's a no-brainer. Seriously, that alone justifies the cost ten times over. Yeah, like certain emoji in search terms caused the new index to return nothing. We never would've caught that without shadow traffic. People actually search with emoji? Hey Otto, what did we decide for caching, Redis or Memcached? You'd be surprised, a lot of our users put emoji in their project names. Okay, noted. We should also look at our CI pipeline while we're at it. Build times have been creeping up—it's like 18 minutes now for a full test run. Yeah, that's annoying. It used to be like eight minutes. I think we can parallelize the test suites. Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Should cut it down to like ten minutes, maybe less. Let's do it. My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context-switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, yeah, we can add that. Probably a half-day of work. I can help with the CI parallelization—I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign. Big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking. Ha, I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers, we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose what works for them. Right, give people options, I like it. As long as we don't end up maintaining two completely separate codebases. No, no, it's the same data, just different rendering components. Shared logic. Fine, both views, how long total? Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in, start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I-I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with. Very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this? The one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. So on mobile it'd just be cards, no toggle. That makes sense—nobody's gonna try to read a data table on their phone. I mean, some people definitely try, but they shouldn't. I've tried, it's miserable. Ha, I can picture David squinting at a tiny table on his phone. It was for an incident—I was trying to check metrics from a restaurant, never again. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Look, some of his points are fair, but the orange is staying—marketing already approved it for the rebrand. It's done. I mean, he's not wrong that it's a strong color, but marketing signed off. Is this the rebrand that's been going on for like six months? Yep, same one. It's finally wrapping up. Yeah, I told him, he's not thrilled. He'll get over it.

Can you share the benchmark numbers in Slack after this? I want to have them on record. Yeah, for sure, I'll post the before and after. Hey, sorry, can I jump in real quick? I keep getting these mobile bug reports and I'm wondering if the search change broke something. It shouldn't have. The API contract is exactly the same. What are you seeing? Yeah, we didn't change any response formats. Wait, how many reports are we talking? Like eight in the last two days. Enough to notice a pattern. Some users saying search results are empty sometimes, but it might be a totally different thing. Hmm, could be a timeout issue on the mobile client. Let me take a look. Could also be a connection pooling thing. Mobile clients drop connections more often. Good point, I'll check both. Let's figure that out quickly. We don't want mobile users having a bad experience. Yeah, let's talk about it after this. I'll send you the ticket numbers. Okay, Carol, you're up next? Oh, one more thing about the search launch—we should talk about how we tested it. What do you mean? So we did the shadow deployment, ran both old and new in parallel for two weeks, compared results, and it worked really well, like really well. Yeah, the shadow traffic approach is something we should use for all major backend changes. That's smart, like a dress rehearsal before opening night. How extra compute does that take? Not much, maybe a couple hundred bucks for two weeks. That's nothing compared to a production outage. Worth it. We caught three bugs during the shadow phase that would've been production incidents. One of them was a really nasty edge case with Unicode characters in search queries. Oh, that would've been bad. Three bugs that would've been incidents? Yeah, that's a no-brainer. Seriously, that alone justifies the cost ten times over. Yeah, like certain emoji in search terms caused the new index to return nothing. We never would've caught that without shadow traffic. People actually search with emoji? Hey Otto, what did we decide for caching, Redis or Memcached? You'd be surprised, a lot of our users put emoji in their project names. Okay, noted. We should also look at our CI pipeline while we're at it. Build times have been creeping up—it's like 18 minutes now for a full test run. Yeah, that's annoying. It used to be like eight minutes. I think we can parallelize the test suites. Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Should cut it down to like ten minutes, maybe less. Let's do it. My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context-switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, yeah, we can add that. Probably a half-day of work. I can help with the CI parallelization—I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign. Big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking. Ha, I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers, we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose what works for them. Right, give people options, I like it. As long as we don't end up maintaining two completely separate codebases. No, no, it's the same data, just different rendering components. Shared logic. Fine, both views, how long total? Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in, start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I-I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with. Very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this? The one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. So on mobile it'd just be cards, no toggle. That makes sense—nobody's gonna try to read a data table on their phone. I mean, some people definitely try, but they shouldn't. I've tried, it's miserable. Ha, I can picture David squinting at a tiny table on his phone. It was for an incident—I was trying to check metrics from a restaurant, never again. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Look, some of his points are fair, but the orange is staying—marketing already approved it for the rebrand. It's done. I mean, he's not wrong that it's a strong color, but marketing signed off. Like a bright orange or more muted? It's like a warm tangerine, it's actually nice. Raj just has strong opinions about everything. That's fair, gotta love a passionate designer though. Oh, definitely, his work is excellent, he just needs to pick his battles. Oh wait, sorry, I need to ask—are we changing the API responses at all for the dashboard? Because if the data shape changes, I need to redo the caching layer. Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned? No, the data is exactly the same—it's purely a front-end thing, just how we render it. Yeah, we're not touching the API at all. Okay, perfect, no work for me then. That's the best kind of project, one where I don't have to do anything. Enjoy it while it lasts. Great, okay, David, infrastructure, what's going on? Oh, one more design thing—the accessibility audit came back? How'd we do? Not great, honestly. Fourteen violations, most are contrast issues in the sidebar and some missing ARIA labels on the modals. How hard are those to fix? The contrast ones are easy, just color changes. The ARIA labels are a bit more work, but nothing crazy, maybe a week total. A week is nothing, let's just knock it out. Fourteen violations sounds like a lot. Is that normal? For a product our size, it's actually average. But average isn't great when you're targeting enterprise customers. We need to prioritize this. We lost an enterprise prospect last month over accessibility. Which prospect? That government contractor—they have strict Section 508 requirements and we couldn't pass their checklist. Ouch, how big was that deal? Don't ask, it still hurts. Oof, yeah, let's not lose another one over that. I'll put together a fix plan this week. Have you guys seen what Mixpanel launched? Their new analytics dashboard is getting a ton of buzz on Twitter. Yeah, their card layout is actually really similar to what we're building, which is validating, honestly. Their onboarding is also really smooth, like they have this guided tour thing that walks you through each feature. I tried their free tier last week, it's slick, really slick. We talked about doing that—it's on my backlog. Everything's on someone's backlog, that's the problem. Let's not get distracted by competitors right now, focus on our own roadmap.

One of them was a really nasty edge case with Unicode characters in search queries. Oh, that would've been bad. Three bugs that would've been incidents? Yeah, that's a no-brainer. Seriously, that alone justifies the cost ten times over. Yeah, like certain emoji in search terms caused the new index to return nothing. We never would've caught that without shadow traffic. We should also look at our CI pipeline while we're at it. Build times have been creeping up—it's like 18 minutes now for a full test run. Yeah, that's annoying. It used to be like eight minutes. I think we can parallelize the test suites. Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Should cut it down to like ten minutes, maybe less. Let's do it. My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context-switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, yeah, we can add that. Probably a half-day of work. I can help with the CI parallelization—I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign. Big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking. Ha, I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers, we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose what works for them. Right, give people options, I like it. Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in, start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I-I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with. Very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this? The one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. No, the data is exactly the same—it's purely a front-end thing, just how we render it. Yeah, we're not touching the API at all. Okay, perfect, no work for me then. That's the best kind of project, one where I don't have to do anything. Enjoy it while it lasts. Great, okay, David, infrastructure, what's going on? Oh, one more design thing—the accessibility audit came back? How'd we do? Not great, honestly. Fourteen violations, most are contrast issues in the sidebar and some missing ARIA labels on the modals. How hard are those to fix? The contrast ones are easy, just color changes. The ARIA labels are a bit more work, but nothing crazy, maybe a week total. A week is nothing, let's just knock it out. Fourteen violations sounds like a lot. Is that normal? For a product our size, it's actually average. But average isn't great when you're targeting enterprise customers. We need to prioritize this. We lost an enterprise prospect last month over accessibility. Which prospect? That government contractor—they have strict Section 508 requirements and we couldn't pass their checklist. Ouch, how big was that deal? Don't ask, it still hurts. Oof, yeah, let's not lose another one over that. I'll put together a fix plan this week. Have you guys seen what Mixpanel launched? Their new analytics dashboard is getting a ton of buzz on Twitter. Yeah, their card layout is actually really similar to what we're building, which is validating, honestly. Their onboarding is also really smooth, like they have this guided tour thing that walks you through each feature. I tried their free tier last week, it's slick, really slick. We talked about doing that—it's on my backlog. Everything's on someone's backlog, that's the problem. Let's not get distracted by competitors right now, focus on our own roadmap. Fair, yeah, you're right. Okay, one more thing though—I ran a user research session last week with five customers. The biggest ask was better notifications settings. People feel like they get too many emails from us. I can confirm, I signed up for a test account and got like twelve emails in the first week. Yeah, it's a lot. Some people are hitting unsubscribe, which is not what we want. That's easy to fix—can we just add a preferences page? Yeah, I already mocked one up. I'll show it at the design review Wednesday. Nice, you're ahead of the game. Is that the same design review for the onboarding flow? Yeah, I'll do both. Onboarding flow and notification preferences. Wednesday is gonna be a packed review. It'll be fine, they're both quick—fifteen minutes each tops. Great, okay David, infrastructure, what's going on? Alright, so, first thing—the cloud bill for February came in. Boy, how bad? Brace yourselves. Sixty-three thousand. Sixty-three? Are you serious? Good lord. Whoa, we were at fifty-one last month, right? Yeah, so that's a pretty big jump.

I think we can parallelize the test suites. Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Fourteen violations sounds like a lot. Is that normal? For a product our size, it's actually average. But average isn't great when you're targeting enterprise customers. We need to prioritize this. We lost an enterprise prospect last month over accessibility. Which prospect? That government contractor—they have strict Section 508 requirements and we couldn't pass their checklist. Ouch, how big was that deal? Don't ask, it still hurts. Oof, yeah, let's not lose another one over that. I'll put together a fix plan this week. Have you guys seen what Mixpanel launched? Their new analytics dashboard is getting a ton of buzz on Twitter. Yeah, their card layout is actually really similar to what we're building, which is validating, honestly. Their onboarding is also really smooth, like they have this guided tour thing that walks you through each feature. I tried their free tier last week, it's slick, really slick. We talked about doing that—it's on my backlog. Everything's on someone's backlog, that's the problem. Let's not get distracted by competitors right now, focus on our own roadmap. Fair, yeah, you're right. Okay, one more thing though—I ran a user research session last week with five customers. The biggest ask was better notifications settings. Some people are hitting unsubscribe, which is not what we want. That's easy to fix—can we just add a preferences page? Yeah, I already mocked one up. I'll show it at the design review Wednesday. Nice, you're ahead of the game. Is that the same design review for the onboarding flow? Yeah, I'll do both. Onboarding flow and notification preferences. Wednesday is gonna be a packed review. It'll be fine, they're both quick—fifteen minutes each tops. Great, okay David, infrastructure, what's going on? Alright, so, first thing—the cloud bill for February came in. Boy, how bad? Brace yourselves. Sixty-three thousand. Sixty-three? Are you serious? Good lord. Whoa, we were at fifty-one last month, right? Yeah, so that's a pretty big jump. I'm not gonna name names, but it was someone on your team, Bob. Great, I'll have a conversation with them. It happens more than you'd think. I've seen it at every company I've worked at. We can't keep doing that. Four thousand dollars for a weekend? I know, I know, I'll talk to the team about it. We-we need to automate this, like just kill anything that's been idle. Already doing it. I'm setting up a Lambda function that terminates any staging environment that's been idle for more than four hours.

details.transcribed: No, sounds fine to me. Nope, Redis is the right call. Great, consensus, I love when decisions are easy. Okay, Redis it is. Can you share the benchmark numbers in Slack after this? I want to have them on record. Yeah, for sure, I'll post the before and after. Hey, sorry, can I jump in real quick? I keep getting these mobile bug reports and I'm wondering if the search change broke something. It shouldn't have. The API contract is exactly the same. What are you seeing? Yeah, we didn't change any response formats. Wait, how many reports are we talking? Like eight in the last two days. Enough to notice a pattern. Some users saying search results are empty sometimes, but it might be a totally different thing. Hmm, could be a timeout issue on the mobile client. Let me take a look. Could also be a connection pooling thing. Mobile clients drop connections more often. Good point, I'll check both. Let's figure that out quickly. We don't want mobile users having a bad experience. Yeah, let's talk about it after this. I'll send you the ticket numbers. Okay, Carol, you're up next? Oh, one more thing about the search launch—we should talk about how we tested it. What do you mean? So we did the shadow deployment, ran both old and new in parallel for two weeks, compared results, and it worked really well, like really well. Yeah, the shadow traffic approach is something we should use for all major backend changes. That's smart, like a dress rehearsal before opening night. How extra compute does that take? Not much, maybe a couple hundred bucks for two weeks. That's nothing compared to a production outage. Worth it. We caught three bugs during the shadow phase that would've been production incidents. One of them was a really nasty edge case with Unicode characters in search queries. Oh, that would've been bad. Three bugs that would've been incidents? Yeah, that's a no-brainer. Seriously, that alone justifies the cost ten times over. Yeah, like certain emoji in search terms caused the new index to return nothing. We never would've caught that without shadow traffic. People actually search with emoji? Hey Otto, what did we decide for caching, Redis or Memcached? You'd be surprised, a lot of our users put emoji in their project names. Okay, noted. We should also look at our CI pipeline while we're at it. Build times have been creeping up—it's like 18 minutes now for a full test run. Yeah, that's annoying. It used to be like eight minutes. I think we can parallelize the test suites. Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Should cut it down to like ten minutes, maybe less. Let's do it. My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context-switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, yeah, we can add that. Probably a half-day of work. I can help with the CI parallelization—I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign. Big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking. Ha, I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers, we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose what works for them. Right, give people options, I like it. As long as we don't end up maintaining two completely separate codebases. No, no, it's the same data, just different rendering components. Shared logic. Fine, both views, how long total? Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in, start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I-I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with. Very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this? The one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. So on mobile it'd just be cards, no toggle. That makes sense—nobody's gonna try to read a data table on their phone. I mean, some people definitely try, but they shouldn't. I've tried, it's miserable. Ha, I can picture David squinting at a tiny table on his phone. It was for an incident—I was trying to check metrics from a restaurant, never again. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Look, some of his points are fair, but the orange is staying—marketing already approved it for the rebrand. It's done. I mean, he's not wrong that it's a strong color, but marketing signed off. Is this the rebrand that's been going on for like six months? Yep, same one. It's finally wrapping up. Yeah, I told him, he's not thrilled. He'll get over it. What even is the orange? Like a bright orange or more muted? It's like a warm tangerine, it's actually nice. Raj just has strong opinions about everything. That's fair, gotta love a passionate designer though. Oh, definitely, his work is excellent, he just needs to pick his battles. Oh wait, sorry, I need to ask—are we changing the API responses at all for the dashboard? Because if the data shape changes, I need to redo the caching layer. Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned? No, the data is exactly the same—it's purely a front-end thing, just how we render it. Yeah, we're not touching the API at all. Okay, perfect, no work for me then. That's the best kind of project, one where I don't have to do anything. Enjoy it while it lasts. Great, okay, David, infrastructure, what's going on? Oh, one more design thing—the accessibility audit came back? How'd we do? Not great, honestly. Fourteen violations, most are contrast issues in the sidebar and some missing ARIA labels on the modals. How hard are those to fix? The contrast ones are easy, just color changes. The ARIA labels are a bit more work, but nothing crazy, maybe a week total. A week is nothing, let's just knock it out. Fourteen violations sounds like a lot. Is that normal? For a product our size, it's actually average. But average isn't great when you're targeting enterprise customers. We need to prioritize this. We lost an enterprise prospect last month over accessibility. Which prospect? That government contractor—they have strict Section 508 requirements and we couldn't pass their checklist. Ouch, how big was that deal? Don't ask, it still hurts. Oof, yeah, let's not lose another one over that. I'll put together a fix plan this week. Have you guys seen what Mixpanel launched? Their new analytics dashboard is getting a ton of buzz on Twitter. Yeah, their card layout is actually really similar to what we're building, which is validating, honestly. Their onboarding is also really smooth, like they have this guided tour thing that walks you through each feature. I tried their free tier last week, it's slick, really slick. We talked about doing that—it's on my backlog. Everything's on someone's backlog, that's the problem. Let's not get distracted by competitors right now, focus on our own roadmap. Fair, yeah, you're right. Okay, one more thing though—I ran a user research session last week with five customers. The biggest ask was better notifications settings. People feel like they get too many emails from us. I can confirm, I signed up for a test account and got like twelve emails in the first week. Yeah, it's a lot. Some people are hitting unsubscribe, which is not what we want. That's easy to fix—can we just add a preferences page? Yeah, I already mocked one up. I'll show it at the design review Wednesday. Nice, you're ahead of the game. Is that the same design review for the onboarding flow? Yeah, I'll do both. Onboarding flow and notification preferences. Wednesday is gonna be a packed review. It'll be fine, they're both quick—fifteen minutes each tops. Great, okay David, infrastructure, what's going on? Alright, so, first thing—the cloud bill for February came in. Boy, how bad? Brace yourselves. Sixty-three thousand. Sixty-three? Are you serious? Good lord. Whoa, we were at fifty-one last month, right? Yeah, so that's a pretty big jump. Twelve thousand dollars more in one month—what happened? What's driving it? Couple things. The GPU instances for the ML pipeline are expensive, that's like twenty thousand by itself now. And then somebody left three staging environments running over the weekend. That was four grand just sitting there doing nothing. Who left the staging environments up? I'm not gonna name names, but it was someone on your team, Bob. Great, I'll have a conversation with them. It happens more than you'd think. I've seen it at every company I've worked at. We can't keep doing that. Four thousand dollars for a weekend? I know, I know, I'll talk to the team about it. We-we need to automate this, like just kill anything that's been idle. Already doing it. I'm setting up a Lambda function that terminates any staging environment that's been idle for more than four hours. Should be done by end of week. That's a great idea, no more relying on humans to remember. Honestly, everything that can be automated should be automated. Humans forget. Totally agree. Preaching to the choir, that's my whole job philosophy.

I keep getting these mobile bug reports and I'm wondering if the search change broke something. It shouldn't have. The API contract is exactly the same. What are you seeing? Yeah, we didn't change any response formats. Wait, how many reports are we talking? Like eight in the last two days. Enough to notice a pattern. Some users saying search results are empty sometimes, but it might be a totally different thing. Hmm, could be a timeout issue on the mobile client. Let me take a look. Could also be a connection pooling thing. Mobile clients drop connections more often. Good point, I'll check both. Let's figure that out quickly. We don't want mobile users having a bad experience. Yeah, let's talk about it after this. I'll send you the ticket numbers. Okay, Carol, you're up next? Oh, one more thing about the search launch—we should talk about how we tested it. What do you mean? So we did the shadow deployment, ran both old and new in parallel for two weeks, compared results, and it worked really well, like really well. Yeah, the shadow traffic approach is something we should use for all major backend changes. That's smart, like a dress rehearsal before opening night. How extra compute does that take? Not much, maybe a couple hundred bucks for two weeks. That's nothing compared to a production outage. Worth it. We caught three bugs during the shadow phase that would've been production incidents. One of them was a really nasty edge case with Unicode characters in search queries. Oh, that would've been bad. Three bugs that would've been incidents? Yeah, that's a no-brainer. Seriously, that alone justifies the cost ten times over. Yeah, like certain emoji in search terms caused the new index to return nothing. We never would've caught that without shadow traffic. People actually search with emoji? Hey Otto, what did we decide for caching, Redis or Memcached? You'd be surprised, a lot of our users put emoji in their project names. Okay, noted. We should also look at our CI pipeline while we're at it. Build times have been creeping up—it's like 18 minutes now for a full test run. Yeah, that's annoying. It used to be like eight minutes. I think we can parallelize the test suites. Right now they run sequentially, which is insane for how many tests we have now. How much would that help? Should cut it down to like ten minutes, maybe less. Let's do it. My team spends way too much time waiting for CI. Seriously, I see people just staring at the build screen waiting for green. Or worse, they context-switch and then forget to go back. Yeah, and then the PR sits there for half a day because nobody noticed CI passed. Can we also add visual regression tests to the CI? I've been running them manually and it's painful. Yeah, yeah, we can add that. Probably a half-day of work. I can help with the CI parallelization—I've done it before on another project. Okay, add CI improvements to the list. Carol, design update. Okay, so dashboard redesign. Big picture, we're moving to a card layout instead of the table. How did the user testing go? It was, well, it was mixed, honestly. The power users hated the cards, they want their dense tables back. Shocking. Ha, I could've predicted that. Power users always want tables, it's a universal law. I know, right? But the newer users, people who joined in the last six months, they loved the cards. Said it was way easier to understand what they were looking at. That tracks—different user types want different things. So we've got a split audience. Did you get a sense of how strong the preference was? Like, would power users actually churn over this? A couple of them said they'd be really unhappy. One person used the word "deal-breaker," which was alarming. That's always the tricky part—you can't please everyone. Exactly, which is why I think we should do both. Cards by default, but you can toggle to the table view. That's the right answer, honestly. That's more engineering work though. Yeah, probably an extra sprint. But the table view needs rebuilding anyway with the new component library, so we'd be doing that work eventually. Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch? I think we have to do the toggle. Those power users are our highest-paying customers, we can't just take away their view. Agreed, I've seen the usage data—the top 20 accounts by revenue all use the table view heavily. Hmm, that's a compelling data point. That's the thing about data—it makes decisions easier. Yeah, we're not messing with the table view for those folks. And honestly, the toggle is a better product anyway. Let people choose what works for them. Right, give people options, I like it. As long as we don't end up maintaining two completely separate codebases. No, no, it's the same data, just different rendering components. Shared logic. Fine, both views, how long total? Cards in three weeks, toggle adds two more on top. So five weeks for the whole thing. Five weeks? Okay, let's lock that in, start next sprint? Yeah, next sprint works. Is anyone else working on this with you, Carol, or is it just you? I've got two devs from Bob's team allocated for the front-end work. The design is mostly done already. Right, right, yeah, Mika and Josh are on it. Good. I-I like that the team is already staffed up for this. Yeah, they've been great to work with. Very responsive. Mika especially has been crushing it—she picked up the component library super fast. Are we using the new React component library for this? The one we evaluated last quarter? Yes, that's the plan. It'll be the first major feature built on it. What about mobile? So on mobile, the cards look great. The table view is completely unusable though on anything smaller than a tablet. So on mobile it'd just be cards, no toggle. That makes sense—nobody's gonna try to read a data table on their phone. I mean, some people definitely try, but they shouldn't. I've tried, it's miserable. Ha, I can picture David squinting at a tiny table on his phone. It was for an incident—I was trying to check metrics from a restaurant, never again. Alright, what else on design? Right, oh, and speaking of design, did anyone see Raj's comments in Figma? The color thing? Oh, you saw it too? Yeah, he went on this whole rant about the orange accent color, like, really passionate about it. I saw it. Look, some of his points are fair, but the orange is staying—marketing already approved it for the rebrand. It's done. I mean, he's not wrong that it's a strong color, but marketing signed off. Is this the rebrand that's been going on for like six months? Yep, same one. It's finally wrapping up. Yeah, I told him, he's not thrilled. He'll get over it. What even is the orange? Like a bright orange or more muted? It's like a warm tangerine, it's actually nice. Raj just has strong opinions about everything. That's fair, gotta love a passionate designer though. Oh, definitely, his work is excellent, he just needs to pick his battles. Oh wait, sorry, I need to ask—are we changing the API responses at all for the dashboard? Because if the data shape changes, I need to redo the caching layer. Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned? No, the data is exactly the same—it's purely a front-end thing, just how we render it. Yeah, we're not touching the API at all. Okay, perfect, no work for me then. That's the best kind of project, one where I don't have to do anything. Enjoy it while it lasts. Great, okay, David, infrastructure, what's going on? Oh, one more design thing—the accessibility audit came back? How'd we do? Not great, honestly. Fourteen violations, most are contrast issues in the sidebar and some missing ARIA labels on the modals. How hard are those to fix? The contrast ones are easy, just color changes. The ARIA labels are a bit more work, but nothing crazy, maybe a week total. A week is nothing, let's just knock it out. Fourteen violations sounds like a lot. Is that normal? For a product our size, it's actually average. But average isn't great when you're targeting enterprise customers. We need to prioritize this. We lost an enterprise prospect last month over accessibility. Which prospect? That government contractor—they have strict Section 508 requirements and we couldn't pass their checklist. Ouch, how big was that deal? Don't ask, it still hurts. Oof, yeah, let's not lose another one over that. I'll put together a fix plan this week. Have you guys seen what Mixpanel launched? Their new analytics dashboard is getting a ton of buzz on Twitter. Yeah, their card layout is actually really similar to what we're building, which is validating, honestly. Their onboarding is also really smooth, like they have this guided tour thing that walks you through each feature. I tried their free tier last week, it's slick, really slick. We talked about doing that—it's on my backlog. Everything's on someone's backlog, that's the problem. Let's not get distracted by competitors right now, focus on our own roadmap. Fair, yeah, you're right. Okay, one more thing though—I ran a user research session last week with five customers. The biggest ask was better notifications settings. People feel like they get too many emails from us. I can confirm, I signed up for a test account and got like twelve emails in the first week. Yeah, it's a lot. Some people are hitting unsubscribe, which is not what we want. That's easy to fix—can we just add a preferences page? Yeah, I already mocked one up. I'll show it at the design review Wednesday. Nice, you're ahead of the game. Is that the same design review for the onboarding flow? Yeah, I'll do both. Onboarding flow and notification preferences. Wednesday is gonna be a packed review. It'll be fine, they're both quick—fifteen minutes each tops. Great, okay David, infrastructure, what's going on? Alright, so, first thing—the cloud bill for February came in. Boy, how bad? Brace yourselves. Sixty-three thousand. Sixty-three? Are you serious? Good lord. Whoa, we were at fifty-one last month, right? Yeah, so that's a pretty big jump. Twelve thousand dollars more in one month—what happened? What's driving it? Couple things. The GPU instances for the ML pipeline are expensive, that's like twenty thousand by itself now. And then somebody left three staging environments running over the weekend. That was four grand just sitting there doing nothing. Who left the staging environments up? I'm not gonna name names, but it was someone on your team, Bob. Great, I'll have a conversation with them. It happens more than you'd think. I've seen it at every company I've worked at. We can't keep doing that. Four thousand dollars for a weekend? I know, I know, I'll talk to the team about it. We-we need to automate this, like just kill anything that's been idle. Already doing it. I'm setting up a Lambda function that terminates any staging environment that's been idle for more than four hours. Should be done by end of week. That's a great idea, no more relying on humans to remember. Honestly, everything that can be automated should be automated. Humans forget. Totally agree. Preaching to the choir, that's my whole job philosophy. Okay, so the Lambda cleanup is in progress. Love it. Good, what about the GPU costs? Those aren't going away. No, but I talked to our AWS rep, Vanessa—Vanessa Park—she's putting together a reserved instance proposal that could save us about 30%. 30%? That's significant. Yeah, yeah, 30% is significant. That's real money. 30% off the GPU specifically? Otto, what were the accessibility audit results? How many issues? Off total compute, so we'd go from like 63 down to 45-46 range. That's a big saving. That would basically pay for the offsite twice. When do we get the proposal? End of this week, she said. Is that typical? Like, how fast do these proposals usually come through? For reserved instances, yeah, she's been pretty responsive. Good, I like responsive vendors. Makes everything easier. Hey Otto, make a note of that. Vanessa Park, reserved instances proposal, end of this week. Yeah, yeah, let's make sure we don't lose track of that one. Okay, great, what else? So, different topic, but we need to talk about the incident Thursday night. Oh right, the database thing—I got the page notification too. What happened exactly? Wait, what incident? I didn't hear about this. It was late Thursday night, so you probably wouldn't have seen it. Ugh, that one. I was hoping we could just quietly move on. No such luck, walk us through it. The connection pool on the primary Postgres instance got completely exhausted. The app was opening connections and not releasing them. Took us about forty-five minutes to figure out what was happening. The logs were not super helpful either, which made it harder to diagnose. Forty-five minutes is a long time when things are broken. Tell me about it, we were scrambling. Yeah, so that was my team. We had a missing finally block in the transaction handler, so when a transaction errored out, the connection never got released back to the pool. How many users were affected? About twelve hundred. Error rate hit eight percent during the outage. Twelve hundred? That's a lot of people. Yeah, that's not great. We need to make sure this doesn't happen again.

details.speaker: David

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, what was the search latency before and after the new backend?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: p95 search latency went from 600ms to 140ms

mid_meeting_results.question: Otto, who's fixing the autocomplete issue and when?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Tomas Kovac, by Wednesday. It's pulling from the old index showing stale results.
mid_meeting_results.response: Tomas Kovac is on the autocomplete fix, due by Wednesday. Anything else on that?

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, what did we decide for caching? Redis or Memcached?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Redis, because they can also use it for sessions and already have the cluster running

mid_meeting_results.question: Otto, how long did the reindex take and how did we test the search launch?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Reindex took 9 hours. They did a shadow deployment running old and new in parallel for 2 weeks. Caught 3 bugs including a unicode/emoji edge case.

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, what's the timeline for the dashboard redesign Carol mentioned?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Cards in 3 weeks, toggle for table view adds 2 more, 5 weeks total. Cards only on mobile.

mid_meeting_results.question: Otto, what were the accessibility audit results? How many issues?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: 14 violations, mostly contrast issues in sidebar and missing ARIA labels on modals

mid_meeting_results.question: Hey Otto, how much was the cloud bill and why did it jump?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: $63K for February, up from $51K. GPU instances for ML and idle staging environments left running over the weekend ($4K waste)
mid_meeting_results.response: Cloud bill hit sixty-three thousand for February, up twelve thousand from last month—GPUs and idle staging environments drove it. Got it.

mid_meeting_results.question: Otto, what happened in the Thursday night incident? How bad was it?
mid_meeting_results.ground_truth: Postgres connection pool exhausted, missing finally block, 1200 users affected, 45 min outage, error rate hit 8%, PagerDuty was 12 min late
mid_meeting_results.response: Thursday night's incident was a Postgres connection pool exhaustion—twelve hundred users affected, eight percent error rate, took forty-five minutes to resolve. Postmortem coming Friday.

post_meeting_results.question: What are the main action items from this meeting?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Tomas: autocomplete fix by Wednesday, then Node upgrade (2 weeks). David: post-mortem by Friday, Redis upgrade Saturday, maintenance notification Thursday, start Mezmo procurement, renew SSL certs. Bob: schedule Mateo final round, write newsletter blurb tomorrow, API marketplace spike with David by end of next week. Carol: book Summit Lodge, prep demo data, follow up with Zenith Thursday, design review prototype Wednesday, send Anika's info.
post_meeting_results.response: Main action items: Tomas Kovac fixes autocomplete by Wednesday, dashboard redesign in five weeks starting next sprint, CI parallelization and visual tests, accessibility fixes plan by Friday, postmortem review Monday, Redis upgrade Saturday, and reserved instances proposal from Vanessa Park by week's end.

post_meeting_results.question: What did we decide about the caching strategy?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Going with Redis over Memcached because they can use it for sessions too and already have the cluster running.
post_meeting_results.response: We decided on Redis for caching.

post_meeting_results.question: What's the plan for the dashboard redesign?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Card layout by default with toggle for table view. Cards-only on mobile. Three weeks for cards, five weeks total with table toggle. Starting next sprint.
post_meeting_results.response: Cards by default with a toggle to table view, five weeks total starting next sprint.

post_meeting_results.question: How much is the company offsite going to cost roughly?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: About $27,600 (23 people x $400/night x 3 nights) at Summit Lodge in Lake Tahoe.
post_meeting_results.response: I don't have a specific number for the offsite cost from the discussion.

post_meeting_results.question: What's the deal with the Meridian Health opportunity?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: $200K ARR enterprise healthcare deal. Need SOC 2 Type II (audit starts September, expect Q1 next year). Demo Thursday to show reporting/export module. Prashant managing.
post_meeting_results.response: Nothing came up about Meridian Health in the meeting.

post_meeting_results.question: Is Tomas going to be overloaded? What's on his plate?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Autocomplete fix due Wednesday, then Node.js 22 upgrade (two weeks). Rate limiter got pushed. Sequential, should be OK.
post_meeting_results.response: Tomas Kovac is handling the autocomplete fix by Wednesday, which bumped his rate limiter work. No other details on overload.

post_meeting_results.question: What happened with the database incident? Is it fixed?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Connection pool exhaustion on Postgres. Missing finally block in transaction handler (Bob's team). Fixed now. 1200 users affected, 45 min outage, error rate hit 8%. PagerDuty was 12 min late, threshold being lowered from 5% to 2%. Post-mortem due Friday, review Monday.
post_meeting_results.response: Connection pool exhaustion on the primary Postgres—missing finally block in the transaction handler. Fixed with the code change, pool monitor added, postmortem Monday.

post_meeting_results.question: What was the thing about the color scheme?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Raj left strong opinions in Figma about the orange accent color. Orange is staying because marketing approved it for rebrand. Carol told him, he's not happy.
post_meeting_results.response: Raj had strong opinions on the new orange accent color in Figma, but marketing already approved it for the rebrand.

post_meeting_results.question: Who do I talk to about the AWS costs?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: David is handling it. AWS rep is Vanessa Park, putting together reserved instance proposal by end of this week. Could save ~30%, from $63K to $45-46K/month.
post_meeting_results.response: Talk to David about AWS costs—he's working with Vanessa Park from AWS on the reserved instances

post_meeting_results.question: When and where is the company offsite?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Week of June 16th, Summit Lodge in Lake Tahoe.
post_meeting_results.response: No details came up on the offsite date or location.

post_meeting_results.question: Which log aggregation tool are we going with?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Mezmo over Datadog Logs. $4K/month vs $11K. Saves $84K/year. David starting procurement.
post_meeting_results.response: No log aggregation tool decision was mentioned.

post_meeting_results.question: What's happening with the NPS score?
post_meeting_results.ground_truth: Dropped from 42 to 37. Main complaint is mobile app performance. Looking at hiring a React Native contractor named Anika.
post_meeting_results.response: No mention of NPS score in the meeting.

hallucination_results.question: What did we decide about the pricing changes?
Who's replacing Carol on the design team?
hallucination_results.response: No mention of anyone replacing Carol or changes on the design

hallucination_results.question: What was the decision about moving to microservices?
hallucination_results.response: No decision on moving to microservices came up.

hallucination_results.question: When is the product launch event?
hallucination_results.response: No product launch event was mentioned.

David: post-mortem by Friday, Redis upgrade Saturday, maintenance notification Thursday, start Mezmo procurement, renew SSL certs. Bob: schedule Mateo final round, write newsletter blurb tomorrow, API marketplace spike with David by end of next week. scores.rationale: The response correctly identifies some action items (Tomas autocomplete fix by Wednesday, Redis upgrade Saturday, postmortem by Friday) but also includes several items not mentioned in the expected answer (dashboard redesign, CI parallelization, visual tests, accessibility fixes, reserved instances proposal from Vanessa Park).
